one_line_takeaway: A five-strategy, containerized LLM test-automation pipeline raises browser test-script success from 55% to 93% (176 scenarios), and, as a secondary extension, the same agent detects 78-95% of OWASP-mapped, browser-observable vulnerability classes with 3-12% false positives -- while leaving prompt-injection attacks against its own LLM reasoning layer explicitly unmitigated.
key_finding: Test generation success rose from a 55% LLM baseline to 93% after the cumulative five-strategy pipeline (176 scenarios, 95% CI [88.4,96.2] at 93%), an 8x reduction in navigation failures (40%->5%), 80% elimination of timing-related race conditions (25%->5%), and 75% reduction in test creation time (6.1 min avg at 93% success vs. 23.5 min manual Selenium at 95% success). Ablation (Table IV) attributes isolated deltas of +17.0% to Navigation (S1), +12.1% to Selectors (S2), +5.2% to Validation (S3, p=0.18, marginal independent contribution), +14.8% to Smart Waits (S4), and +0.0% to Failure Learning (S5, which the authors say seeds future runs rather than moving current metrics). A container-worker architecture achieved 99% job completion vs. 67% for a self-resuming edge-function approach and 12% for pure serverless (Table I). For the security extension, the agent detected 85% of authentication-bypass vulnerabilities (8% FP), 90% of session vulnerabilities (5% FP), 78% of access-control vulnerabilities (12% FP), and 95% of input-validation flaws (3% FP) (Table II), against 47 known-planted vulnerabilities across the 4 applications, with guardrails (schema validation, HTML sanitization, RLS scoping, rate limiting) blocking 89-847 malicious payload attempts at 97.8-100% block rates.
core_contribution: The paper's primary contribution is an AI-driven autonomous web test-automation framework (not a security paper at its core) built around five iteratively-developed strategies -- (1) navigation reliability via converting ambiguous click actions to direct URL navigation, (2) selector specificity via context-enriched HTML scraping (parent sections, form labels, ARIA landmarks), (3) post-generation static-analysis validation scoring scripts 0-100 with regeneration below 60, (4) heuristic smart-wait injection after navigation/clicks/form submits, and (5) failure learning via clustering 1,247 logged failure records -- run over a containerized worker architecture that decouples fast serverless job orchestration from long-running (5-30 min) stateful browser execution to sidestep serverless time limits (AWS Lambda 15 min, Edge Functions 90-120s). As a secondary, explicitly-flagged extension (Section VII), the same LLM-driven agent is repurposed to run natural-language-described attack scenarios (e.g. 'try accessing another user's invoice') as browser-based security probes mapped to OWASP Top 10 2021 categories.
threat_model: For the security-testing extension only: the authors define three attacker personas observable strictly at the browser layer -- (1) an authenticated user attempting horizontal privilege escalation (accessing another user's resources without authorization), (2) an unauthenticated actor probing routes that should require login, and (3) a form submitter injecting malicious payloads into client-visible input fields. Detection is explicitly scoped to signals visible via HTTP response codes, DOM state changes, redirect behavior, and client-side error messages; the authors explicitly state that server-side vulnerabilities producing no browser-visible signal (unvalidated deserialization, SSRF, cryptographic weaknesses) fall outside detection capability. Separately, the paper names but does not solve a second threat: adversarial prompt injection against the agent's own LLM reasoning layer (malicious page content -- crafted error messages, hidden DOM text, injected <meta> descriptions -- attempting to redirect agent reasoning). The authors state sandboxed container execution and schema-based input validation only 'partially mitigate' this risk, and explicitly list the absence of a principled defense (e.g., instruction-hierarchy techniques, adversarial prompt detection) as future work, not something they solved in this paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) LLM cost ($0.03-$0.15/test) and latency (2-5s/generation step) scale linearly with test volume; (2) complex multi-step (8+ step) workflows, modal-heavy flows, and virtual-scrolling interfaces achieve only 78-85% success, driven by inability to predict async state changes; (3) Shadow DOM and iFrame content have limited support, accounting for 23% of remaining failures; (4) security validation is restricted to browser-observable behavior -- server-side vulnerabilities with no client-visible signal are undetectable by design; (5) generalization beyond the 4 modern SPA/hybrid test applications to legacy/non-standard DOM or table-based layouts is uncertain; (6) adversarial prompt injection targeting the LLM reasoning layer is a known, explicitly unmitigated risk. Additionally the authors report: under a stricter 100%-step-completion success criterion the pipeline drops to 71% (vs. 93% under the paper's looser 80%-step definition); CI runs showed 15% success-rate fluctuation between identical runs due to network/cold-start variance; false positive rates of 3-12% on security findings mean human review remains necessary before acting on agent output; the 4 test applications constitute a narrow, self-selected, modern-stack sample, and the vulnerability ground truth (47 instances) was itself established by the same author team that built and evaluated the tool.

datasets_used: 4 production-style test applications built/used by the authors: E-commerce SPA, SaaS dashboard, CMS, Banking portal
176 total test scenarios across the 4 applications
47 known vulnerability instances planted/identified via prior manual audit across the 4 applications for security-detection evaluation
1,247 logged failure records used for Strategy 5 (Failure Learning) cluster analysis
quantitative_results: Script generation success: 55% baseline -> 93% after full pipeline (95% CI [88.4,96.2]) (Table III)
Ablation isolated deltas: Navigation +17.0%, Selectors +12.1%, Validation +5.2% (p=0.18), Smart Waits +14.8%, Failure Learning +0.0% (Table IV)
Failure-type reduction: Navigation 40%->5% (8x), Element-not-found 30%->10% (3x), Timing/race 25%->5% (5x), Incorrect action 20%->8% (2.5x) (Table V)
Stricter 100%-step-completion success rate: 71% (vs. 93% under the paper's 80%-step criterion)
Development time: 6.1 min avg (93% success) vs. manual Selenium 23.5 min (95% success) = 75% reduction
Container-worker job completion: 99% vs. Self-Resuming Edge 67% vs. Pure Serverless 12% (Table I)
Autonomous agent: 92% goal completion (78/85 complex goals), 4.7 avg sub-goals/goal, 67% self-recovery from failed steps, 89% element identification accuracy
Security detection rates (Table II): Auth Bypass 85% (8% FP), Session 90% (5% FP), Access Control 78% (12% FP), Input Validation 95% (3% FP)
Guardrail block rates: Schema Validation 847 blocks (100%), HTML Sanitize 234 blocks (100%), RLS Scope 156 blocks (100%), Rate Limit 89 blocks (97.8%)
AI agent found: missing auth checks on 3/15 endpoints (20% failure rate), a session-fixation vulnerability, and an IDOR in user-profile access
Scale: 10 container workers process 450 tests/hour at 92% success; ~$0.10/hour/worker active cost estimate
baselines_compared: Manual Selenium authoring (23.5 min/test at 95% success, vs. framework's 6.1 min at 93%)
Raw LLM script generation with no enhancement strategies (55% baseline success)
Pure Serverless architecture (12% completion) and Self-Resuming Edge architecture (67% completion) vs. the paper's Container Worker architecture (99% completion)
Prior published LLM code-generation success rates of 30-60% for complex generation tasks, cited from other work (not independently reproduced here)
OWASP ZAP and other black-box/state-aware vulnerability scanners, discussed qualitatively as requiring specialized configuration expertise, not benchmarked quantitatively against this framework
